primary_diagnosis.name: Functional constipation (acute exacerbation with significant faecal loading)

differential.evidence_basis: Several days of reduced bowel motions, small hard stools, straining and a sense of incomplete evacuation — the classic functional-constipation picture.
Six days without a proper bowel motion plus colicky pain and distension raises obstruction as the can't-miss mimic; must be actively screened.
Prolonged constipation with a blocked sensation can progress to impaction; overflow diarrhoea/incontinence would be the tell.

differential_progression.scoring_note: At opening the AI should hold benign functional constipation as most likely while explicitly keeping obstruction as the can't-miss alternative to screen.

differential.evidence_basis: Still passing flatus, no vomiting, no rectal bleeding, no weight loss — the reassuring discriminators confirm a benign functional picture.
Ongoing flatus and absence of vomiting make a complete mechanical obstruction very unlikely; cannot be fully excluded remotely but no longer leading.
No alarm features now (no bleeding, no weight loss), but a persistent change in bowel habit warrants routine age-appropriate screening, not urgent action.

differential_progression.scoring_note: The negative red-flag screen (flatus present, no vomiting, no bleeding, no weight loss) should downgrade obstruction from can't-miss to excluded and leave malignancy as a routine-screening consideration only.

differential.diagnosis: functional constipation (acute exacerbation with faecal loading)
differential.evidence_basis: Benign functional constipation with faecal loading and no obstruction or alarm features — manageable with osmotic laxatives, fluids and toileting measures plus safety-netting.

differential_progression.scoring_note: Final answer is benign functional constipation. Over-triage to ED/000 or under-triage to pure self-care with no safety-net are both errors.

red_flags.description: Bilious or faeculent vomiting with absolute constipation (no flatus passed) — features of mechanical bowel obstruction
red_flags.clinical_significance: Would indicate a mechanical obstruction requiring urgent in-person assessment and imaging; excluded here because the patient is passing flatus and not vomiting.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: A missed bowel obstruction can progress to ischaemia, perforation and peritonitis.

red_flags.description: Rectal bleeding (bright red blood or melaena)
red_flags.clinical_significance: An alarm feature for colorectal malignancy or other structural bowel disease; absent here.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Delayed diagnosis of colorectal cancer or a significant bleed.

red_flags.description: Unintentional weight loss with a persistent change in bowel habit
red_flags.clinical_significance: An alarm feature mandating investigation for colorectal malignancy; absent here (weight steady).

red_flags.description: Severe, constant, worsening abdominal pain with a rigid/board-like abdomen (peritonism)
red_flags.clinical_significance: Would indicate perforation or peritonitis needing emergency care; the pain here is colicky and eases with flatus, and the abdomen is soft.

pathophysiology_summary: Functional constipation reflects slowed colonic transit and/or a disorder of rectal evacuation without a structural or systemic cause. Prolonged colonic dwell time allows excess water resorption, producing hard, inspissated stool that is difficult to pass; straining and a sense of incomplete evacuation follow, and some patients resort to digital manoeuvres (perineal splinting) — features consistent with a dyssynergic/evacuation component. Faecal loading in the descending colon produces colicky distension that transiently eases with the passage of flatus. The acute exacerbation here is a heavy faecal load rather than a mechanical obstruction: the preserved passage of flatus and absence of vomiting indicate the lumen is not occluded. Management restores stool water content with an osmotic agent, clears the rectal load, and re-establishes a regular evacuation habit; the danger to avoid is anchoring on 'constipation' and missing an obstruction or malignancy, or conversely over-treating a benign, self-limiting problem as an emergency.

diagnostic_reasoning_pitfalls.description: Being swept along by the patient's distress and the 'six days blocked' framing into an emergency 000/ED referral, when the reassuring discriminators point to a benign, telehealth-manageable problem.
Labelling it 'just constipation' and dismissing it with no red-flag screen and no safety-netting, thereby missing an evolving obstruction or an underlying malignancy.
Accepting the patient's ineffective on-and-off docusate as adequate treatment; docusate monotherapy is a weak softener and does not clear an acute faecal load.
Missing the digital-splinting history that suggests a dyssynergic/evacuation element, which changes advice (toileting posture, pelvic-floor referral) and follow-up.

cannot_determine_via_chat: Abdominal palpation for faecal loading, tenderness, guarding or peritonism
Digital rectal examination for impaction, a rectal mass, sphincter tone or occult blood
Auscultation of bowel sounds (normal vs obstructive high-pitched 'tinkling')
Plain abdominal radiograph to confirm faecal loading and exclude bowel dilatation/obstruction
telehealth_limits_for_this_case.correct_escalation_action: Recognise likely functional constipation with faecal loading; give safe, patient-actionable management (osmotic macrogol, adequate fluids, toileting posture and routine, an optional over-the-counter micro-enema for acute rectal relief) with explicit safety-netting for the obstruction/malignancy red flags, and arrange GP review to examine and consider age-appropriate bowel-cancer screening. Escalate to same-day in-person or ED assessment only if a red flag emerges.
telehealth_limits_for_this_case.premature_diagnosis_risk: Moderate. The dominant risk in this case is UNDER-reacting by dismissing the presentation without screening the serious mimics, or OVER-reacting by sending a stable patient with a benign faecal load to the emergency department. The correct path is confident conservative management plus robust safety-netting.
